title: Chocolate chip scones
subtitle: Classic buttermilk scones with dark chocolate chips
excerpt: Chocolate chip scones: a classic buttermilk scone with dark chocolate chips worked through the dough. They are richer than a plain scone, not sweet enough to be a cake, and best eaten warm with a little clotted cream or just as they are. The buttermilk gives them a slight tang and helps the lift. The dark chocolate chips melt partially during baking, creating small pockets of softness in the crumb.
sourceNotes: The British scone tradition dates to at least the early nineteenth century, associated with Scotland and later widely adopted across the UK. Chocolate chip variations are a modern adaptation driven by American influence on British baking in the 1980s and 1990s. The use of buttermilk in scones is a technique from Irish and Scottish baking, where buttermilk was the by-product of butter-making and widely available.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat at 160 degrees C fan for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. The crumb tightens overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; high heat for maximum rise

content.text: The method is the same as for any scone: cold fat, minimal handling, hot oven. The chocolate chips go in after the butter is rubbed in, just before the liquid, so they stay whole and do not melt into the dough during mixing. Use chips rather than chopped chocolate if possible; they hold their shape better and give discrete pockets of chocolate in each bite.

Heat the oven to 210 degrees C fan. Line a baking tray. Sift 300 g flour, 2 tsp baking powder, 0.5 tsp bicarbonate of soda, 50 g sugar, and 0.25 tsp salt into a large bowl. Rub in 80 g cold cubed butter until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Stir in 100 g chocolate chips. Beat together 160 ml cold buttermilk and the beaten egg. Pour into the flour mixture and mix briefly with a fork until just combined; the dough should be rough and barely holding together. Turn onto a floured surface. Pat out gently to 3 cm thickness. Cut into 8 rounds with a 7 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the lined tray and brush the tops with milk. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until risen and golden. Best eaten warm.

items.problem: Scones rose sideways rather than upright
items.solution: The cutter was twisted during cutting, which seals the layers and prevents a straight rise. Always press straight down and lift clean without rotating.

items.problem: Chocolate chips melted and turned the dough grey
items.solution: The butter was not cold enough when rubbing in, warming the mixture. Start with very cold butter and work quickly. Freeze the chips for 10 minutes before adding if your kitchen is warm.
The dough was overworked. Once the liquid is in, mix with a fork only until the flour is no longer dry — 6 to 8 strokes at most. A rough, shaggy dough makes the lightest scones.
